narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 36 (slightly under the 36.7 average), Year 1 grade decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 1, and plans to attend full-time—and calculated a 47.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize candidates with stronger signals of bar success. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your Year 3 grade decile of 1, which is in the bottom tier compared to the average student's 5.53 decile—this low performance signaled potential challenges in sustaining academic rigor through law school and beyond. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your LSAT score, which had a meaningful positive influence by nudging the prediction toward bar passage, as it's close to the solid average and shows good test-taking ability. Your family's upper-middle income also helped a bit, pulling slightly in the direction of success, likely because it correlates with access to resources that aid bar preparation in the dataset. Overall, your Year 3 grades stood out as the top driver of concern, outweighing the supportive roles of your LSAT and family background, especially when viewed against peers who typically rank much higher in later-year deciles. Your full-time enrollment plans align with the vast majority of students (over 92%), so they didn't sway the outcome much. While your undergraduate GPA was another below-average area, it wasn't among the top influences here. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a stable background—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic decile through stronger coursework or retakes, perhaps retaking the LSAT to push it above average, and consider programs offering bar prep support or part-time options if that fits better. Gathering letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience could also strengthen future applications, and exploring post-baccalaureate programs might rebuild your GPA profile. With these steps, you can reshape how models like this see your bar exam odds.
